title: Linux Privesc — Vector by Priority
body: Check vectors in order of deterministic+quiet → exploit+risky. sudo/GTFObins first (instant, no crash), then SUID/caps, then cron/service, then kernel LAST (can panic the box and kill your shell).
cmd:
# LINUX PRIVESC PRIORITY LADDER - check in THIS order, stop at first win.

# 1) SUDO RULE (quickest, zero risk) - ALWAYS first:
sudo -l
#   Any NOPASSWD entry? → GTFObins escape for that binary = instant root.
#   BEYOND: (ALL) NOPASSWD: /usr/bin/git → sudo git -p help → !/bin/bash
#   The moment you see a sudo rule, you can almost skip the rest.

# 2) SUID / CAPABILITIES (deterministic):
find / -perm -4000 -type f 2>/dev/null
getcap -r / 2>/dev/null
#   Match the binary on GTFObins → SUID escape.

# 3) CRON / WRITABLE SCRIPT / WEAK SERVICE (misconfig, reliable):
cat /etc/crontab ; ls -la /etc/cron.*
#   Writable script run by root = root.

# 4) PASSWORD REUSE (cheap, overlooked):
#   configs, history, .ssh - a looted password may just su to root.

# 5) KERNEL EXPLOIT - LAST RESORT:
#   linpeas flagged DirtyPipe etc. on BEYOND - you did NOT need them.
#   Kernel exploits can PANIC the host and kill your shell. Only after 1-4.
warn: linpeas dumps every candidate at once - that's noise, not a priority list. Work the ladder top-down: a NOPASSWD sudo rule beats a kernel CVE every time. Kernel exploits are last because they can crash the host.

choices.label: sudo -l has an entry — GTFObin it
